cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why Ms. Leibfried asked the author to recite a passage in front of the class. The choices are A to D. First, I need to go back to the text to recall the relevant part. The author mentions that the big project was a book report to be read aloud, but Ms. Leibfried suggested she do something "a little different" and recite a passage from "The Bell Jar." The context around this is that the author was shy, the only non-white student, and felt out of place. Ms. Leibfried noticed her interest in reading and writing and helped her feel less shy. The act of reciting helped the author gain confidence. So why did the teacher suggest this? Let's look at the options. Option A is testing memory. The story doesn't mention memory tests. The focus was on reading and presenting a passage, not on memorization. The book report was part of a class project, but the key here is the personal encouragement. Option B is about earning respect. The author did receive applause after, but the teacher's main goal seems more about boosting the student's confidence. The author was shy and needed encouragement, so maybe the teacher wanted to help her come out of her shell, not just to make her earn respect from peers. Option C is to deepen understanding of "The Bell Jar." The text says the author picked a passage she considered the best, but there's no mention of the teacher's intent being to deepen understanding. It might have been a part of the project, but the teacher's specific suggestion seems more personal. Option D is to guide her to build relationships. The author was isolated, and the teacher might have wanted to help her engage with classmates. However, the immediate effect was the author gaining confidence, not necessarily building relationships.
